However, it expired on 3 April, and the EU parliament decided not to vote to extend it, amid privacy concerns from some lawmakers. lead_story.title: Trump's blockade threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
lead_story.summary: The threat to block the Strait of Hormuz sharpens the standoff with Iran and raises the chance of miscalculation, while leaving the underlying strategic problems unresolved. For energy markets, allies and shipping, the immediate question is how much real enforcement capability Washington can bring to bear without widening the conflict.
lead_story.source_signal_title: Trump's blockade threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
body: Donald Trump’s threat to blockade the Strait of Hormuz has sharpened a confrontation with Iran that was already edging into dangerous territory. It also underscores a familiar problem in Middle East crises: the rhetoric can move faster than the capability to carry it out.

The strait is one of the world’s most important shipping lanes, carrying a large share of global oil and gas exports. Even a partial disruption can jolt energy markets, raise insurance costs and force shipping companies to rethink routes and risk management.

But a blockade is not a simple switch Washington can turn on. It would depend on sustained naval enforcement, cooperation from allies and an ability to prevent retaliation against ships, bases and regional infrastructure — all without triggering a broader war.

That is why the threat carries as much danger as leverage. If US forces begin intercepting vessels or pressing tanker operators to comply, the chances of miscalculation rise sharply, especially with Iranian commanders under pressure to show they can still respond.

For allies in Europe and Asia, the immediate concern is less about the legality of the language than the practical consequences of escalation. Energy-importing economies are already exposed to higher prices, and shipping firms have little appetite for a standoff that could turn the strait into a live conflict zone.

Yet the underlying strategic dilemmas remain unchanged. Washington still wants to pressure Tehran without being dragged into a wider regional war; Iran still wants to use disruption as leverage without inviting overwhelming retaliation. The threat may harden positions, but it does not resolve the conflict that produced them.

stories.title: Civilians feared killed after reports of air strike on Nigerian market
stories.summary: Reports of a strike on a village market in Nigeria raise urgent questions about civilian harm, targeting and accountability as the military has not addressed the allegations.
stories.source_signal_title: Civilians feared killed after reports of air strike on Nigerian market
body: Reports from north-east Nigeria say an air strike hit a village market while the military was targeting suspected militants nearby. If confirmed, it would add to long-running concerns about the protection of civilians in Nigeria’s conflict zone.

The Nigerian Air Force has not publicly addressed the allegations. That silence has left open basic questions about what was struck, whether there was a targeting error and how many people may have been killed or injured.

The area has for years seen operations against Islamist insurgents, especially in Borno and neighbouring states, where civilian populations often live close to front lines and military targets. In such settings, distinguishing militants from civilians can be difficult — but that does not remove the obligation to account for harm.

What matters now is whether the authorities investigate openly and explain what happened. Without that, claims of collateral damage risk deepening distrust among communities that already fear both insurgents and the security forces sent to fight them.

stories.title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
stories.summary: The failure of the talks leaves the ceasefire fragile and the diplomatic track uncertain, with major implications for regional security and global energy flows.
stories.source_signal_title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
body: The collapse of the talks leaves the ceasefire and the wider diplomatic track in an uncertain place. Twenty-one hours of discussions were not enough to bridge the hostility that has defined relations between Iran and the US for decades.

That matters because the alternative to diplomacy is not stability but drift: a tense stand-off in which both sides test the limits of the ceasefire while trying to avoid being blamed for breaking it. In the region, that is often how fragile truces begin to unravel.

What happens next will depend on whether the US and Iran can keep channels open, and whether regional actors can restrain their allies and proxies. The risks are not only military but economic, because any renewed disruption around the Gulf quickly feeds into oil markets and shipping.

stories.title: Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations
stories.summary: Both sides say the other has breached the truce repeatedly, underscoring how quickly pauses in fighting can unravel and how little trust remains.
stories.source_signal_title: Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations
body: Ukraine and Russia have accused each other of carrying out hundreds of ceasefire violations, underlining how unstable even a short pause in fighting can be. The claims point to a truce that is already under severe strain.

President Volodymyr Zelensky said Ukrainian forces would respond symmetrically to Russian attacks, signalling that Kyiv does not intend to absorb fire without replying. Moscow, for its part, has also accused Ukraine of breaking the terms of the arrangement.

The dispute reflects a deeper problem: neither side trusts the other to observe limits on the battlefield, and each sees the truce as a tactical move rather than the start of a political settlement. If that remains the case, any pause may be brief and contested.

stories.title: At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site
stories.summary: A crush at a tourist event in Haiti has left dozens feared dead, adding to the country’s long running security and public safety crisis.
stories.source_signal_title: At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site
body: At least 30 people are feared dead after a crush at a tourist event in Haiti, according to officials. The incident adds another layer to the country’s prolonged crisis of insecurity, weak public services and limited emergency response capacity.

The crush is believed to have occurred at a popular site crowded with young people, making the toll especially alarming. In a country where large gatherings can quickly become dangerous, any mass-casualty incident raises immediate questions about crowd control and rescue arrangements.

Haiti has endured years of gang violence, political instability and repeated humanitarian shocks. That wider backdrop matters because it leaves little margin for managing accidents of this kind, and it means families often face delayed medical care and limited official information after disasters.

Authorities have yet to provide a full account of the cause, the number of injured or whether the site was properly secured. Those answers will be crucial both for accountability and for understanding whether this was a tragic accident or a preventable failure.

stories.title: Djibouti's president wins unprecedented sixth term with 97.8% of vote
stories.summary: Ismail Omar Guelleh has extended his long rule in an election boycotted by the main opposition, again highlighting the country’s constrained political competition.
stories.source_signal_title: Djibouti's president wins unprecedented sixth term with 97.8% of vote
body: Djibouti’s president, Ismail Omar Guelleh, has won an unprecedented sixth term after an election in which the main opposition was absent. The scale of the result reflects not only his long hold on power but also the weakness of political competition in the small Horn of Africa state.

Guelleh has now extended a rule that began more than two decades ago, while critics have long argued that opposition space in Djibouti is tightly constrained. Boycotts and limited contestation have made recent elections more about ratifying the status quo than testing it.

Djibouti matters well beyond its borders because of its strategic location at the mouth of the Red Sea and its role as a host to foreign military bases. That gives the country outsized geopolitical significance, even as domestic politics remain tightly managed.

stories.title: Artemis crew home safely after completing historic mission to the Moon
stories.summary: Four astronauts have returned safely after a nine-day mission that took them farther from Earth than any humans have been, marking a major milestone for the programme.
stories.source_signal_title: Artemis crew home safely after completing historic mission to the Moon
body: Four astronauts have returned safely to Earth after completing a nine-day Artemis mission that took them farther from the planet than any humans have yet travelled. Their splashdown marks a milestone for a programme that is intended to take NASA back to the Moon on a sustained basis.

The mission matters not only for its symbolism but also for the testing it gives NASA on spacecraft systems, crew safety and deep-space operations. Each successful flight helps build confidence for longer journeys that will eventually be needed if the agency is to reach the lunar surface and beyond.

For now, the safe return is the immediate achievement. The next question is how quickly the programme can move from demonstration to routine missions, and whether funding, technical setbacks or shifting political priorities could slow that path.

stories.title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
stories.summary: With tankers stranded and oil prices exposed to geopolitical risk, investors are weighing whether the disruption turns into a broader hit to growth and inflation.
stories.source_signal_title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
body: The collapse of US-Iran talks has left markets facing a longer period of energy risk rather than a clean diplomatic reset. With tanker traffic constrained and the Strait of Hormuz exposed to further disruption, investors are having to price in the possibility of a sustained shock.

That is important because oil and gas remain central to inflation expectations, shipping costs and consumer confidence. Even when prices ease from immediate peaks, the effects of a geopolitical disruption can take months to work through the system.

The bigger worry for markets is that the disruption becomes structural rather than temporary. If shipping companies avoid the strait, insurers raise premiums and governments struggle to restore confidence, the impact could spread beyond energy into growth forecasts and corporate earnings.

For now, traders are trying to separate a temporary spike from a broader macro hit. The problem is that the politics remain unsettled, which means the market damage could outlast any single flare-up in the conflict.

stories.title: What is a naval blockade and how would it work in Strait of Hormuz?
stories.summary: A practical guide to how a blockade might be attempted, what it could and could not achieve, and why the legal and military risks are so high.
stories.source_signal_title: What is a naval blockade and how would it work in Strait of Hormuz?
body: A naval blockade is an attempt to stop ships entering or leaving an area by using naval forces, surveillance and the threat of interception. In the Strait of Hormuz, that would mean trying to control one of the world’s most sensitive choke points, where even brief disruption can affect global trade.

In practice, a blockade would be difficult to enforce. Warships would need to identify vessels, issue warnings, stop or search them and deal with any challenge from Iran or from other states whose ships are caught up in the operation. That raises the risk of rapid escalation.

The legal position is also contested, especially if no broader international mandate exists. Even if Washington argued the move was necessary for security, allies, tanker operators and coastal states would need to decide whether to treat it as legitimate or as an unlawful act of coercion.

stories.title: Soaring pump prices drive US inflation to highest level in almost two years
stories.summary: Higher fuel costs linked to the Iran war have lifted US inflation, strengthening expectations that the shock is feeding through into the wider economy.
stories.source_signal_title: Soaring pump prices drive US inflation to highest level in almost two years
body: Higher fuel prices linked to the Iran conflict have pushed US inflation to its highest level in almost two years. The rise at the pump is feeding into the wider economy at a moment when households are already sensitive to the cost of living.

The immediate effect is straightforward: motorists pay more, transport costs rise and businesses that depend on fuel face tighter margins. Over time, that can feed through to food, travel and other everyday prices, making the inflation shock harder to dismiss as temporary.

The question for policymakers is whether this is a one-off energy spike or the start of a broader inflationary spell. If the strait remains constrained and oil prices stay elevated, the pressure on the Federal Reserve and the White House is likely to intensify.

stories.title: EU airline industry warns of fuel shortages if Strait of Hormuz stays closed
stories.summary: European airlines are warning that restricted access to jet fuel could begin to affect operations if shipping through the strait remains constrained.
stories.source_signal_title: EU airline industry warns of fuel shortages if Strait of Hormuz stays closed
body: European airlines are warning that restricted access to jet fuel could begin to hit operations if shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains constrained. The concern is not just about price, but about availability and supply timing.

Airlines are among the industries most exposed to fuel disruptions because they rely on steady deliveries and relatively predictable costs. If refiners and shippers cannot move product freely from the Gulf, carriers may have to absorb higher prices or pass them on to passengers.

The warning is another sign that the conflict’s effects are reaching well beyond the battlefield. Even where flights continue, prolonged fuel strain could force airlines to rethink schedules, routes and hedging strategies if the disruption persists.

stories.title: How many ships are crossing the Strait of Hormuz?
stories.summary: BBC Verify’s analysis suggests only a few vessels are crossing, giving a sense of how severely traffic has been disrupted by the standoff.
stories.source_signal_title: How many ships are crossing the Strait of Hormuz?
body: BBC Verify’s analysis suggests only a few vessels are currently crossing the Strait of Hormuz, a sharp sign of how disrupted traffic has become since the ceasefire deal. That kind of slowdown points to caution from ship operators rather than a return to normality.

The figure matters because the strait is one of the world’s most important maritime routes. Even a temporary drop in traffic can unsettle energy markets, delay deliveries and increase pressure on insurers and port authorities.

The next issue is whether traffic resumes once shipowners judge the risk acceptable, or whether the standoff keeps the corridor effectively constrained. Until that becomes clearer, every crossing will be read as a test of how much control either side really has.

stories.title: Will UK petrol and diesel prices start going down?
stories.summary: Fuel costs in Britain have risen on the back of the conflict; the ceasefire may ease pressure, but the timing and scale of any fall remain uncertain.
stories.source_signal_title: Will UK petrol and diesel prices start going down?
body: Fuel costs in Britain have risen sharply because of the conflict in the Middle East, leaving drivers waiting to see whether the ceasefire will bring relief at the pumps. The answer depends on how quickly wholesale oil and refined fuel prices settle.

In principle, lower crude prices should eventually feed through to petrol and diesel. But that process is neither immediate nor guaranteed, because retailers, wholesalers and forecourts all adjust at different speeds and may be cautious if markets remain volatile.

For households, the key question is timing as much as direction. If the Strait of Hormuz stays open and shipping risk falls, prices should ease; if uncertainty returns, any drop could be delayed or very limited.

stories.title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
stories.summary: Calls for state ownership reflect the scale of uncertainty around the company’s future and the wider policy problem of keeping strategic industry viable.
stories.source_signal_title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
body: British Steel is facing renewed pressure over its future, with one Labour MP arguing that nationalisation may be the best outcome by the summer. The comment reflects how far the company’s troubles go beyond a single ownership dispute and into questions of industrial strategy.

The steelmaker has long symbolised Britain’s difficulty in keeping heavy industry viable in a competitive global market. Energy costs, ageing infrastructure and uncertain investment have all made private rescue plans harder to sustain.

For ministers, the issue is not simply whether to intervene but how far. Any move towards state ownership would raise questions about cost, management and precedent, but inaction could leave a strategically important industry facing a further slide into uncertainty.

stories.title: OpenAI pauses UK data centre deal over energy costs and regulation
stories.summary: The pause underlines the gap between ambitious AI investment rhetoric and the realities of power availability, regulation and project economics.
stories.source_signal_title: OpenAI pauses UK data centre deal over energy costs and regulation
body: OpenAI has paused a planned UK data centre deal, citing pressures around energy costs and regulation. The decision is a reminder that even the biggest AI ambitions still depend on practical questions such as power supply, planning rules and project economics.

Data centres are among the most energy-intensive pieces of digital infrastructure, and the UK has been trying to attract more of them as part of its push to become a serious AI hub. But the scale of that ambition runs into a familiar constraint: the electricity grid is not always ready for it.

The pause will worry policymakers who have marketed the UK as open for frontier-tech investment. It also suggests that the race for AI infrastructure is not just about chips and talent, but about whether countries can provide cheap, reliable power at the scale the industry wants.

stories.title: Faisal Islam: Why the government is relaxed about Chinese car imports
stories.summary: The government’s stance suggests it sees cheap Chinese EVs as a consumer gain and an industrial challenge rather than an outright threat.
stories.source_signal_title: Faisal Islam: Why the government is relaxed about Chinese car imports
body: The government appears relaxed about the rise of Chinese car imports because it sees them as both a consumer benefit and an industrial challenge. Cheap electric vehicles can help households and accelerate the shift away from petrol and diesel, even if they intensify pressure on domestic manufacturers.

That stance reflects a broader dilemma for ministers: protecting strategic industry versus giving consumers lower prices and more choice. On balance, the government seems to believe that blocking imports would do more harm than good, especially while the UK is trying to expand EV adoption.

The unanswered question is whether domestic producers can adapt quickly enough. If Chinese brands continue to gain ground, Britain may need to decide whether support should focus on competitiveness, resilience and supply chains rather than trying to hold back the market.

stories.title: Tice £91,000 tax row is 'minor administrative error', party claims
stories.summary: Reform UK is seeking to contain the political damage from allegations around a property company linked to its deputy leader and unpaid dividend tax.
stories.source_signal_title: Tice £91,000 tax row is 'minor administrative error', party claims
body: Reform UK is trying to contain the fallout after reports that a property company linked to its deputy leader failed to pay £91,000 in dividend tax. The party says the issue amounts to a minor administrative error, but the row is politically awkward for a party that has made tax and waste central to its message.

The case matters because it touches on a familiar vulnerability for politicians who campaign on accountability. Even when the sums are limited, tax disputes tend to raise questions about judgment, compliance and whether leaders apply the rules they demand of others.

Reform will want to avoid letting the issue widen into a broader story about standards or financial opacity. Whether that happens will depend on what further details emerge and how convincingly the party can explain the lapse.

stories.title: Starmer urges US and Iran to 'find way through' after peace talks falter
stories.summary: The prime minister is warning against escalation after the collapse of talks, while trying to keep the UK clear of direct involvement in the conflict.
stories.source_signal_title: Starmer urges US and Iran to 'find way through' after peace talks falter
body: Sir Keir Starmer has urged the US and Iran to “find a way through” after talks collapsed without agreement, warning against further escalation. His intervention reflects Britain’s interest in keeping the crisis contained while avoiding direct involvement.

The prime minister’s comments also show how much the UK is trying to balance its alliance with Washington against the risks of a wider regional conflict. That is a familiar position for Britain, which has often backed de-escalation in the Gulf while relying on US military power for deterrence.

The uncertainty now is whether diplomacy can be revived quickly enough to prevent fresh disruption to shipping and energy markets. If it cannot, European allies may face growing pressure to respond to a crisis they do not control.

stories.title: Votes for populist parties in May elections will put NHS at risk, Streeting says
stories.summary: Labour is trying to frame the local and devolved elections around the NHS, arguing that protest votes could weaken support for the health service.
stories.source_signal_title: Votes for populist parties in May elections will put NHS at risk, Streeting says
body: Wes Streeting is seeking to make the NHS the central issue in the May elections, warning that votes for populist parties could put the health service at risk. Labour’s argument is that protest voting carries real consequences, not just symbolic ones.

The strategy reflects the continuing political power of the NHS in Britain, where trust in public services often shapes local and national voting. It also shows Labour trying to cast its rivals as a threat to stability rather than simply an outlet for dissatisfaction.

Whether that message lands will depend on whether voters see the health service as improving or still under strain. If waiting lists, staffing and morale remain dominant concerns, the NHS is likely to stay at the centre of the campaign.

stories.title: Home Office to announce closure of 11 asylum hotels in next week
stories.summary: The planned closures are part of Labour’s wider pledge to end the use of asylum hotels, though the political and logistical pressures remain substantial.
stories.source_signal_title: Home Office to announce closure of 11 asylum hotels in next week
body: The Home Office is set to announce the closure of 11 asylum hotels, a move that forms part of Labour’s pledge to end the use of hotels for asylum accommodation. The announcement will be welcomed by ministers who want to show progress on one of the most politically charged issues in migration policy.

But closing hotels is only part of the challenge. The government still has to find alternative accommodation for people whose claims are unresolved, while managing pressure from councils, local residents and campaigners over where they will be housed.

The question next is whether the closures represent genuine momentum or a reshuffling of the same problem. Unless the asylum system speeds up decisions and reduces backlogs, the pressure on the wider estate is likely to remain.

stories.title: Britain could adopt single market rules without MPs’ vote as part of UK-EU reset
stories.summary: Ministers are considering legislation that could deepen alignment with EU rules, raising fresh questions over parliamentary scrutiny and sovereignty.
stories.source_signal_title: Britain could adopt single market rules without MPs’ vote as part of UK-EU reset
body: Ministers are considering legislation that could allow Britain to adopt single market rules without a fresh vote by MPs, as part of a wider reset with the EU. The idea would deepen regulatory alignment, but it immediately raises questions about parliamentary scrutiny and sovereignty.

The proposal reflects a broader shift in post-Brexit politics: the practical benefits of closer alignment are increasingly being weighed against the symbolic costs. For businesses, more consistent rules could reduce friction; for critics, the democratic issue is whether such changes should be made explicitly in Parliament.

How far the government goes will depend on the final shape of the reset and the political appetite for confrontation. Any move seen as softening Brexit too quietly is likely to meet resistance, even if it improves trade relations.

stories.title: ‘Too powerful for the public’: Inside Anthropic’s bid to win the AI publicity war
stories.summary: Anthropic’s decision not to release a model has become a reputational and regulatory campaign, as firms compete to define AI safety, power and legitimacy.
stories.source_signal_title: ‘Too powerful for the public’: Inside Anthropic’s bid to win the AI publicity war
body: Anthropic has made a strategic choice not to release one of its models, turning a technical decision into a broader argument about power, safety and legitimacy. In doing so, it is trying to position itself as the company that takes AI risks more seriously than its rivals.

That approach matters because the AI industry is fighting for public trust at the same time as regulators are asking harder questions about security, labour disruption and abuse. A firm that appears cautious may win credibility, but it also risks looking evasive or elitist.

The publicity battle is therefore not just about branding. It is about who gets to define what responsible AI looks like, who sets the standards and whether safety claims are backed by transparent evidence or simply by messaging.
Anthropic’s challenge is that restraint can be read in two ways: as prudence or as a marketing tactic. As the technology becomes more capable, that distinction is likely to matter more, not less.

stories.title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
stories.summary: The industry is trying to shift public debate through policy messaging, but public scepticism, regulatory scrutiny and product risks are all rising.
stories.source_signal_title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
body: AI companies are trying to improve their standing by funding policy papers, research groups and thinktanks. The aim is to shape the debate around safety, productivity and regulation before governments set the terms for them.

But there is a limit to how far policy messaging can repair public scepticism. The sector is still facing criticism over job losses, misinformation, copyright disputes and the concentration of power in a small number of firms.

That leaves companies with a difficult choice: engage more openly with regulators and critics, or risk looking as though they are trying to buy influence. Funding research may help with access and credibility, but it will not by itself solve the industry’s trust deficit.

stories.title: ‘Irresponsible failure’: Google, Meta, Snap and Microsoft slam EU over child sexual abuse law lapse
stories.summary: Tech groups and child safety advocates are clashing over a legal gap that may reduce the detection of abuse material and grooming online.
stories.source_signal_title: ‘Irresponsible failure’: Google, Meta, Snap and Microsoft slam EU over child sexual abuse law lapse
body: Major technology companies and child safety advocates are clashing over an EU legal gap that could affect the detection of child sexual abuse material and grooming online. The dispute centres on how to preserve protections without creating blanket surveillance powers.

The companies argue that the lapse risks making it harder to identify abusive content. Critics of the regulatory framework, however, have long warned that emergency powers or overbroad scanning requirements can threaten privacy and lawful communication.

The issue matters because it sits at the fault line between two public interests that are both hard to compromise: keeping children safe and preserving digital rights. The next steps from Brussels will determine whether the gap is closed quickly or becomes a wider political fight.

stories.title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
stories.summary: Brainwave and avatar technology has enabled a performer living with motor neurone disease to appear on stage again, showing a more humane use of advanced tech.
stories.source_signal_title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
body: A dancer living with motor neurone disease has been able to perform on stage again through digital avatar technology, using brainwave systems to recreate movement and expression. The result is striking not only as a technical achievement but as a human one.

The story matters because it shows how advanced technology can be used for restoration rather than novelty. For people who lose physical control but remain fully aware and creative, tools that recover some form of performance can have profound emotional value.

It also points to the growing overlap between medicine, computing and the arts. As such systems improve, they may offer new ways for people with severe disabilities to communicate, work and appear in public life.

stories.title: Man City smell blood - why Arsenal should fear title rivals
stories.summary: Manchester City’s win over Chelsea has turned up the pressure on Arsenal, with the title race now feeling far less secure for the leaders.
stories.source_signal_title: Man City smell blood - why Arsenal should fear title rivals
body: Manchester City’s latest win has tightened the Premier League title race and put fresh pressure on Arsenal. After a strong run from the leaders, Pep Guardiola’s side have now made it clear they are closing in.

The significance is not just the three points but the timing. When City start to build momentum, rivals know how quickly a lead can disappear, especially with a game in hand and a squad used to handling late-season pressure.
For Arsenal, the warning is obvious: any slip now invites scrutiny and doubt. In a title race, that can matter as much as the numbers on the table.

stories.title: Will Romero's tears become the enduring image of Tottenham's season?
stories.summary: Tottenham’s defeat at Sunderland has deepened fears of relegation and raised the prospect of a defining image for a season gone wrong.
stories.source_signal_title: Will Romero's tears become the enduring image of Tottenham's season?
body: Cristian Romero’s tears after Tottenham’s defeat at Sunderland may become the defining image of a season that has gone badly wrong. With Spurs now in the relegation zone, the symbolism of the moment is hard to ignore.

The result matters because it deepens the sense that the club is not merely out of form but in crisis. Defensive mistakes, managerial upheaval and poor results have combined to leave Tottenham with little room for optimism.

If the season ends in relegation or a desperate fight for survival, Romero’s reaction will likely be remembered as more than an emotional afterthought. It could become the image that sums up the collapse.

stories.title: Van Aert beats Pogacar in thrilling Paris-Roubaix sprint
stories.summary: Wout van Aert has won Paris-Roubaix after edging Tadej Pogacar in a dramatic sprint finish on the cobbles.
stories.source_signal_title: Van Aert beats Pogacar in thrilling Paris-Roubaix sprint
body: Wout van Aert has won Paris-Roubaix after beating Tadej Pogacar in a dramatic sprint finish. The result gives the Belgian one of cycling’s most prized one-day victories on the sport’s roughest terrain.

The race mattered because it pitted two of the biggest names in cycling against each other in a contest that combines endurance, nerve and luck. On the cobbles, even the strongest riders can be undone by a puncture, a crash or a split-second mistake.
Van Aert’s victory will sharpen the rivalry at the top of the sport, while Pogacar’s performance again shows how far he has extended his ambitions beyond stage racing.

stories.title: Sinner beats Alcaraz to return to world top spot
stories.summary: Jannik Sinner has reclaimed world number one after beating Carlos Alcaraz in Monte Carlo, tightening one of tennis’s defining rivalries.
stories.source_signal_title: Sinner beats Alcaraz to return to world top spot
body: Jannik Sinner has returned to world number one after beating Carlos Alcaraz in Monte Carlo. The result adds another chapter to a rivalry that is increasingly shaping the men’s game.

The significance goes beyond ranking points. Sinner and Alcaraz are now the pair most likely to define the next era of men’s tennis, and their meetings are beginning to carry the feel of major turning points rather than isolated matches.
For now, Sinner has the edge and the status that comes with it. The challenge for Alcaraz is to respond quickly, because in a rivalry this tight, momentum can shift fast.
